title: Kundalini and Trust: Learning to Trust a Process That Feels Untrustworthy
meta_description: You're probably reading this because you're in it, aren't you? That feeling of being utterly lost, like your body's betraying you, your mind's a war zone,
body:
Kundalini and Trust: Learning to Trust a Process That Feels Untrustworthy

You're probably reading this because you're in it, aren't you? That feeling of being utterly lost, like your body's betraying you, your mind's a war zone, and everything you thought was real just crumbled into dust. You're scared. I know that fear. It's not just a little anxiety; it's that primal, gut-wrenching terror that makes you question if you're losing your mind, if you're dying, or both. You're lying awake at 3 AM, heart pounding, skin crawling, and you're searching for answers, for someone to tell you you're not crazy. Right? I've been there. I've been exactly where you are, staring at the ceiling, feeling like the world was ending inside me.

This isn't some spiritual joyride you signed up for. This is raw, unvarnished reality hitting you square in the face. You're feeling energies you didn't even know existed, sensations that defy explanation, and thoughts that would make anyone doubt their sanity. And everyone around you? They don't get it. They tell you to just "relax," or "think positive," or worse - they just look at you with pity. You feel utterly alone. You're wondering, "How do I trust this? How do I surrender to something that feels like it's tearing me apart?"

The truth is, it's not easy. It's probably the hardest thing you'll ever do. But you can do it. You have to. Because fighting it just makes it worse. I'm not going to lie to you and say it's all rainbows and unicorns. It's not. It's messy. It's painful. It's confusing. But there's a reason for it, even if you can't see it right now. And learning to trust - even just a tiny bit - is your lifeline.

The Betrayal of the Body and Mind

Let's talk about what's actually happening. Your body feels like it's short-circuiting. You've got these intense vibrations, burning sensations, tingling, electric shocks. Maybe you're having involuntary movements, tremors, or muscle spasms. Your digestion's probably shot, your sleep's a joke, and you're exhausted but wired. Your nervous system is in overdrive, constantly on high alert. It's like someone flipped a switch you didn't even know existed, and now you can't turn it off. This isn't just "stress," you know? This is something way beyond that. You're experiencing a deep, energetic rewiring, and your body is struggling to keep up.

And your mind? Oh man, your mind. It's probably the worst part, right? Intrusive thoughts, existential dread, panic attacks that come out of nowhere. You're questioning everything: your past, your future, your identity, the very fabric of reality. You might be having vivid dreams, nightmares, or even waking visions. You're seeing things differently, feeling things more intensely, and it's overwhelming. You feel like you're losing your grip, like you're detached from yourself, from others, from life itself. It's terrifying because your mind, which used to be your ally, now feels like your worst enemy. It's screaming at you that something is terribly wrong.

This isn't a sign you're broken. It's a sign you're changing. A really, really intense change. Your old self is dying, and a new one is trying to be born. And that process? It's violent. It's disruptive. It doesn't ask for your permission. It just happens. And your job, right now, is to learn how to ride that wave instead of drowning in it. It feels untrustworthy because it's unpredictable, uncontrollable, and it's stripping away everything you thought gave you security. But here's the thing: that stripping away is necessary. It's clearing out the old to make space for the new. It's just doing it in a way that feels like an eviction notice followed by a demolition crew.

What "Trusting the Process" Actually Means

When someone tells you to "trust the process" during kundalini awakening, it probably sounds like a cruel joke. How can you trust something that's causing you so much pain and fear? It's not about blindly believing everything will be okay. It's not about being naive. It's about a very specific kind of trust, a trust that you probably haven't had to develop before. It's a trust that says, "Even though I don't understand this, even though it hurts like hell, I'm going to allow it to move through me, because fighting it makes it worse."

It means letting go of control. That's a big one, isn't it? You've spent your whole life trying to control your environment, your feelings, your thoughts. Now, you're faced with something utterly uncontrollable. And that's terrifying. But what if the control you're grasping onto is actually the very thing prolonging your suffering? What if letting go, even a little, allows the energy to flow more smoothly, to do what it needs to do without resistance?

Trusting the process also means accepting that you don't have all the answers. You won't. Not now, maybe not ever. This isn't a linear path with a clear instruction manual. It's a journey into the unknown. And that's okay. It's okay to not know. It's okay to be confused. It's okay to be scared. What's not okay is to pretend you know, or to resist the mystery because it's uncomfortable. Know what I mean? You're being asked to sit in the discomfort, to be present with it, without judgment.

It's about having faith - not necessarily in a religious sense, but faith in the intelligence of your own being. Faith that there's a deeper wisdom at play here, even if it feels chaotic. Your body knows how to heal. Your energy system knows how to rebalance. It's just doing it in a way that's incredibly intense and messy because you've probably got a lot of old stuff to clear out. And that's where the trust comes in - trusting that this messy, painful clearing is actually serving a higher purpose, even if you can't see the full picture yet.

Practical Steps for Cultivating Trust (When You Feel You Can't)

Okay, so how do you actually do this? It's not like you can just flip a switch and suddenly trust. It's a practice, a daily, moment-to-moment effort. And you're going to fail sometimes. You're going to doubt. You're going to panic. That's normal. Don't beat yourself up for it. Just keep coming back to these things.

  • Acknowledge the Fear: Don't pretend you're not scared. You are. It's okay. Say it out loud. "I'm terrified right now." When you acknowledge it, you take some of its power away. You're not the fear; you're the one observing the fear. That's a big distinction.
  • Ground Yourself: When you're overwhelmed, you're probably disconnected from your body, floating somewhere in a sea of anxiety. Bring yourself back. Feel your feet on the ground. Focus on your breath. Really feel the inhale and the exhale. It sounds simple, but it's incredibly powerful. You can also try holding ice cubes or splashing cold water on your face - anything to shock your system back into the present moment.
  • Limit Information Overload: You're probably endlessly searching online, right? Trying to diagnose yourself, reading horror stories. Stop. Seriously. While it's good to understand what's happening, too much information, especially negative information, will just feed your fear. Find a few trusted resources - like Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link) - and stick to them. Don't go down every rabbit hole.
  • Create a Routine, Any Routine: Even if you feel like your life is spiraling, having some semblance of routine can provide a tiny anchor. Wake up at the same time, eat at the same time, take a walk. Simple, consistent actions can create a feeling of stability when everything else feels unstable.
  • Connect with Nature: Get outside. Even if it's just sitting on a park bench. Feel the sun on your skin, the wind in your hair. Nature has a way of grounding us and reminding us of a larger, calmer rhythm that exists beyond our internal chaos.
  • Express Yourself: You've got a lot bottled up inside. Find a way to get it out. Journaling is incredible for this. Just write whatever comes to mind, no filter. Don't worry if it makes sense. Just let it flow. The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link) is a great tool for this, or even just a simple notebook like a Leuchtturm1917 Hardcover Dotted Notebook - A5 (paid link). You can also try creative outlets like drawing, painting, or even coloring - a Mandala Coloring Book for Adults - Stress Relief (paid link) can be surprisingly calming.
  • Seek Support (the Right Kind): This is crucial. You need someone who understands, or at least believes you. A therapist who specializes in spiritual emergence, a trusted friend who's been through something similar, or a support group. Avoid people who dismiss your experiences or tell you you're crazy. You don't need that right now.

The Role of Surrender and Acceptance

Surrender. It's another one of those words that can feel impossible when you're in the thick of it. It doesn't mean giving up or becoming passive. It means letting go of your resistance to what is happening. It means stopping the internal fight. Imagine you're in a powerful river. You can fight against the current, exhausting yourself, getting battered against rocks. Or you can relax, float, and allow the current to carry you. That's surrender. It's not about liking the current; it's about not resisting it.

Acceptance isn't approval. You don't have to like what's happening to accept that it is happening. You can say, "This is incredibly painful, and I hate it, AND I accept that this is my reality right now." That "and" is everything. It allows you to acknowledge your feelings without getting stuck in them. It's about making peace with the present moment, however uncomfortable it may be. Look, you can't change the fact that this energy is moving through you. You can only change your relationship to it. And acceptance is the first step in changing that relationship.

When you surrender and accept, even in small ways, you create space. Space for the energy to move more freely. Space for insights to arise. Space for healing. It's like unclogging a drain. The more you resist, the more backed up everything gets. The more you allow, the more it can flow. It won't instantly make everything perfect. You'll still have tough days, maybe even tougher days. But you'll find moments of peace, moments where you can breathe, moments where you feel a flicker of trust that this isn't just chaos, but actually a very intense, very personal purification process.

Integration: The Long Road Ahead

This isn't a sprint; it's a marathon. And the goal isn't to "get rid" of kundalini, but to integrate it. To become whole again, but a new kind of whole. Integration means learning to live with this awakened energy, to understand its language, and to use it for your highest good. It means bringing all the pieces of yourself - the light, the dark, the spiritual, the mundane - into harmony. It's a lifelong process, honestly.

You're going to have periods of intense activity, followed by periods of calm. Don't mistake the calm for the end of the journey. It's just a pause, a chance to catch your breath and digest what's happened. During these calmer times, that's when the real work of integration happens. That's when you start to make sense of the experiences, to embody the changes, to rebuild your life on a new foundation. It's not about going back to "normal" because your old normal is gone. It's about creating a new normal, one that's more aligned with your true self.

This process will change everything about you. Your values will shift, your relationships might change, your career might change. You'll see the world with new eyes. And while that can be unsettling, it's also incredibly liberating. You're shedding layers of conditioning, old wounds, limiting beliefs. You're becoming more authentic, more real. And that's something worth trusting, even when the path to get there feels like walking through fire.

So, when you're lying awake tonight, feeling that familiar dread, just try to breathe. Remember that I've been there, and countless others have too. You're not alone, and you're not crazy. This is a real thing, and you're strong enough to get through it. Take it one breath, one moment, one tiny act of surrender at a time. The trust will come, slowly, imperfectly, but it will come. Because deep down, you know there's something bigger happening here. Something that, ultimately, is trying to heal you. And that's what you're learning to trust. Does that land?

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
